chapters.title: A new way to think about the 21st century economy
chapters.text:
Since the start of the 21st century, economics students have called for a change in what they learn. They feel that current theories do not explain the crises we face. Climate change is one crisis, and the syllabus still reflects an industrial age, while the real world has gone digital.

Student protests started the conversation, but a boycott of lectures can only point to the problem. It cannot fix it. You can keep challenging the current setup, but at some point you have to offer an alternative. Doughnut Economics might be the answer we need.

Doughnut Economics lays out seven key ways of thinking we need to embrace to build the economy of the 21st century.

We need a new economic story that fits the world we live in and offers a strong response to the biggest challenges of our time.

Life expectancy has climbed from forty-eight years in 1950 to seventy-one years in 2010, but inequality has soared in the same time. The richest one percent now hold more wealth than the rest of the world combined. On top of that, the planet is heating up, and the United Nations has estimated that the global population will reach eleven billion by 2100.

Economists carry weight, and politicians listen to them when they make policies that touch lives. That is why we need to rethink economics, so 21st century economists can give better advice and push leaders to make choices that help everyone, not just a few. We will look at why and how to change how we measure success, how to fight rising inequality, and how to use wealth to fight ecological damage.

chapters.title: A better way to measure economic progress
chapters.text:
Using GDP as the main measure of progress was the language of the 20th century. GDP hides the cost of producing the output a country brags about. In many cases, that cost is severe inequality and environmental damage. For example, a family of four earning ten thousand dollars a month might look wealthy, but if more than eighty percent of that money comes from one person, the outcome can be painful.

In earlier years, economics was about earning as much as possible. That mindset created a world where billionaires sit on piles of money while others starve. What we need is a yardstick that lets everyone thrive. A world that offers equal opportunity and respects the dignity of work. A world that builds safe communities and cares for the planet. Let us call that world the Doughnut to give it shape.

The idea of Doughnut Economics was first coined in 2011 by economist Kate Raworth to picture the goal of economics for the 21st century.

The 21st century goal of economics should be to meet the needs of every person while protecting the planet.

The Doughnut has two rings. The inner ring is the social foundation of life. It contains twelve basic needs of every person: enough food, a clean environment and clean water, a reliable power supply, good education, decent health care, good housing, decent work and income, social equity, a political voice, peace and justice, gender equality, and access to information.

A shortfall happens when people fall below this social foundation and are pushed into survival mode.

The outer ring of the Doughnut is the ecological ceiling. It is the upper boundary we cannot cross if we want the planet to keep supporting life. Beyond that ceiling are climate change, biodiversity loss, pollution, ozone depletion, and other threats to life on the planet.

We have to keep everyone between the inner and outer rings of the Doughnut. That is the sweet spot.

chapters.title: Bringing every player in the economy to the table
chapters.text:
The world is not static, and neither are its needs. We need to keep up with the changes the world has gone through. To restore balance to the global economy, we have to swap the characters that defined 20th century economics for new ones. Today economists decide what happens to world economies, so the script they are following has to change, and someone has to write a new one with new players who have clear needs and ambitions.

The current script was first written by Paul Samuelson when he designed the Circular Flow diagram in macroeconomics. The diagram shows how money moves around an economy. Over time, it became the basis for defining the economy itself. It painted a picture of an efficient market, the benefits of trade for both sides, the perfection of finance, and the idea that the common person is the problem.

The global crises of the 20th century opened our eyes to the flaws of that model. Money does not just flow between households and businesses in a closed loop, as the diagram suggests. There are leaks and injections from at least three outside sources: banks, government, and international trade.

Their interference means that only some of the money gets counted in the system. Over time, those blind spots lead to chaos.

We need a fairer model that brings all players to the table. This new model, the Embedded Economy, has these traits. A planet that supports life. Social bonds that support humanity. Support for the systems of a diverse economy. Recognition of the work done in households. A market that is powerful but wisely embedded. Room for the creativity of the commons. A state that is accountable. Financial services. Innovative businesses. Fair trade. And protection against the abuse of power.

This new model takes us beyond tracking the flow of income to understanding other forms of wealth the old model ignored.

Our well-being depends on more than money. We need a safe planet, human connections, social capital, and physical health to flourish.

The Embedded Economy pushes us to look past market efficiency and to value the work of households, the state, and cooperative efforts.

chapters.title: Humans are more than just rational actors
chapters.text:
Old economic theory painted people as solitary, calculating, competitive, and never satisfied. That portrait leaves no room for our humanity. At best, it turns us into robots that cannot change their programming. We have to change this image, because we will act the way we see ourselves.

A new self-portrait lets us see ourselves as rational and complex.

Embracing our complexity opens the door to economies that let us live in the safe and just space of Doughnut Economics. There are five main areas where our self-image has to change to reach that goal. These shifts in mindset will bring us closer to global security and prosperity.

First, we have to move from seeing people as purely self-interested to seeing them as social and giving. Observation of human behavior shows that we punish selfishness and reward cooperation. We like to share and dislike people who try to take an unfair advantage. The level of give and take varies across cultures, but the principle shows up everywhere.

Second, we have to accept that our values are not fixed, they are fluid. Since the 1980s, social psychologist Shalom Schwartz and his colleagues have surveyed people from many backgrounds in over eighty countries. They found that humans hold ten basic values that motivate us. Each value can show up when triggered, and we can move from one to another many times a day and across a lifetime.

Third, no one exists in isolation. We need each other to thrive. Our world is interconnected. We have communication systems that tell us what people on the other side of the world are doing in real time. That knowledge shapes our behavior, and it shapes economic choices as well.

Fourth, we are approximating beings, not calculators. Psychologists have found more than one hundred and sixty cognitive biases. We feel more than we think. Policymakers need to keep that in mind when they try to change behavior.

Fifth, we do not stand above nature. We are a small part of the web of life. The old image of nature as a pyramid with humans on top has to give way to a web-like view. We are not rulers. We are participants in protecting and caring for our home.

chapters.title: An economy is more like an organism than a machine
chapters.text:
Instead of the old binary view of supply and demand, we have to dig deeper to find the hidden factors. In the 1600s, when Isaac Newton asked why an apple falls from a tree, he did not worry about how the apple was formed. Economists have taken the same mechanical view of the economy. It is time to treat the economy as a living organism that grows and needs support.

We have to move from a narrow focus on supply and demand to understanding what drives people to produce and consume.

Economists need to see the economy as an apple tree, not just a falling apple. When we look at the economy as a whole, we will find hidden factors that shape the final result, the apple, or the GDP.

As Kate Raworth has put it, today economy is divisive and degenerative by default, and tomorrow economy must be distributive and regenerative by design.

When we look past the surface, what we find will push us to nurture an economy that spreads value across the whole system instead of concentrating it in the hands of a few. We will also adopt practices that support the life-giving cycles of the Earth so that we can keep producing apples on a safe planet.

To make that shift, we need economists who treat the economy like a garden rather than a machine. Their tools will be gardening tools, not wrenches. They will not try to predict and control the economy, but to watch how things are moving and adjust policies to fit the direction of change. Rather than rushing in every time the economy shows stress, we should build diversity and redundancy so it can bounce back on its own.

The word economics was first used by the philosopher Xenophon in Ancient Greece, and it means the art of household management. His work, Oeconomicus, is a Socratic dialogue about household life and farming.

chapters.title: Tackling global inequality
chapters.text:
Inequality is growing as automation takes over more of the economy. Some efforts have eased inequality within countries, but global inequality is still a huge problem. Over the last fifty years, planet-wide income has grown by fifty percent, which sounds good, but the gains have gone mostly to the richest. The poorest have seen only a small lift. That pushes people outside the rings of the Doughnut. The reason is that we have not yet seen ourselves as part of a single global community. What we do in one country affects people in another, and we are connected in more ways than we admit.

So far, global migration has been the biggest way wealth has spread.

Take the example of people who move to high-income countries and send money home to family and friends in low-income countries. The open question is whether wealthy countries will keep welcoming these migrants. It is important that migration does not end up making income gaps worse instead of better.

Today there are better ways to make sure overseas development assistance from rich countries to developing countries reaches the right people. Technology has made mobile banking possible, so people in extreme poverty can get funds directly.

As Kate Raworth has asked, when it comes to giving a basic income to the poorest people on the planet, the question is no longer how on earth, but why on earth not.

Beyond spreading wealth, we have to work together to protect the life-giving systems of the Earth. Resources are not endless, even when we act as if they are. The planet is our common home, and each of us has to protect the part that is in our care, for the good of the world and for the generations that come after us.

We also need a platform for sharing knowledge, so that anyone, anywhere, can get the information they need to solve local problems. Solving problems creates wealth. Knowledge is a privilege many people do not have. As education spreads, the world will become a better, more thriving place to live.

chapters.title: Using wealth to slow ecological damage
chapters.text:
Going green is no longer about looking wealthy. It is about survival. The story that economic growth will fix environmental problems in developing countries is not true. Take India, for example. Economic growth has not led to a cleaner environment there. We have to be intentional about ecological problems, because they will not fix themselves.

Industries are growing fast, draining the planet of resources and steadily ruining it in the process. People can be selfish and forget that Earth is our only home. Once we destroy it, we have nowhere else to go. To change course, we have to accept that the Earth is not our property, even when we treat it that way. We cause harm not only to ourselves but also to other living beings. Corporate greed is destroying natural habitats and pushing many species to the edge of extinction. Big companies in particular have to take responsibility for their actions and put real effort into repairing the harm they have caused.

The economic system of the industrial age was linear and degenerative, not cyclical and regenerative, and that led to the exhaustion of resources.

We have mined the Earth minerals and burned fossil fuels for over two hundred years without planning to give anything back. That goes against how nature works, because in nature everything that takes also gives back. In our case, we give back to pollution and degradation while making ourselves very rich. It is time to design new economic models that put the health of the planet into the equation.

Efforts to push corporations to care about the environment have moved through stages. First, they did nothing. Then they did what paid off. When they could no longer look away, they reluctantly did their share. As environmental pressure grew, they started to focus on avoiding harm. The current stage is generosity. Generosity means committing to regenerative designs that keep us within the safe and just space of Doughnut Economics. We have to learn to cut down on waste of matter and energy by recycling, refurbishing, repairing, and reusing as much as we can.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The shape of our future depends on the kind of economics we practice. Doughnut Economics offers a hopeful blueprint for spreading wealth fairly and protecting the environment. It tells us to set aside the linear view that measures growth only through GDP and to pay attention to other forms of wealth that matter for well-being.

The state of the world may make this goal feel out of reach, but we have to hold on to hope. We have already taken the first step by naming the problem. We know that the economics of the 20th century failed us and added to the damage. Now we have to commit to the proposed solution. We have what it takes to build back better.

Students of economics today should be encouraged to take up this new way of thinking and doing business, not the old methods that no longer serve us. The journey to a safe and just space starts with the recognition that every economy contributes to the safety of everyone. No one is isolated. We have to value the contribution of every stakeholder: households, the state, the market, and the commons. We have to help them work together to build economies that support life and let everyone flourish.

We have to start seeing the economy as an organism that needs us as gardeners, not as a machine that needs technicians. That is how we will build economies that support life without being hooked on GDP.

Depending on where you are in the world, ask yourself what choices you can start making that will spread wealth more fairly and slow down the planet damage.
